chunks.text: Apollo Architecture Document  1. Overview  Apollo is a next-generation microservices platform designed to handle real-time data processing at scale. It leverages event-driven architecture, container orchestration, and distributed tracing to provide a robust foundation for modern applications.  1.1 Key Design Goals  - Scalability: Horizontal scaling to handle 100K+ requests per second - Resilience: Self-healing with circuit breakers and automatic failover - Observability: Distributed tracing with OpenTelemetry integration - Security: Zero-trust network with mTLS between all services  1.2 Technology Stack  2. Authentication System  2.1 JWT-Based Authentication  Apollo uses JWT (JSON Web Tokens) for stateless authentication. The authentication flow works as follows: 1. Login: User credentials are sent to the Auth Service 2. Validation: Auth Service validates against the user database 3. Token Generation: A signed JWT is generated with a 15-minute expiry 4. Refresh Token: A long-lived refresh token (7 days) is also issued 5. Token Verification: All subsequent requests verify the JWT via the Auth Service's public key  2.2 API Key Authentication  For machine-to-machine communication, Apollo supports API key authentication: - API keys are hashed using bcrypt before storage - Keys are prefixed with ap_ for identification - Each key has scoped permissions (read, write, admin) - Keys can be revoked individually without affecting other keys  2.3 OAuth 2.0 Integration

Apollo supports OAuth 2.0 with the following grant types: - Authorization Code: For web applications - Client Credentials: For server-to-server communication - Refresh Token: For maintaining long-lived sessions  3. Data Pipeline  3.1 Event Processing  The event processing pipeline handles millions of events per minute: Producer ? Kafka Topic ? Stream Processor ? Sink Connector ? Database Events are serialized using Apache Avro for schema evolution support. Each event carries: - A unique event ID (UUID v7) - Timestamp in RFC 3339 format - Event type and version - Payload in Avro binary format  3.2 Stream Processing  Apache Flink is used for stream processing with the following operators: - Filter: Remove invalid or spam events - Map: Transform event formats - Window: Aggregate events in tumbling windows - Join: Correlate events from multiple streams  3.3 Data Retention  Data retention policies are configurable per topic:  4. Deployment Strategy  4.1 Kubernetes Deployment  Apollo runs on Kubernetes with the following deployment strategy: - Canary Deployments: 10% traffic shifted to new version, then 50%, then 100% - Rolling Updates: Max surge of 25%, max unavailable of 25%

- Blue-Green: Full environment swap for critical services  4.2 CI/CD Pipeline  The CI/CD pipeline is implemented using GitHub Actions: 1. Build: TypeScript compilation + Docker image build 2. Test: Unit tests, integration tests, e2e tests 3. Security Scan: Snyk vulnerability scanning + SAST 4. Deploy: Automated deployment to staging, manual approval for production  4.3 Infrastructure as Code  All infrastructure is defined using Terraform: module "kubernetes_cluster" { source = "./modules/gke" region = "us-west1" node_count = 3 machine_type = "e2-standard-4" }  5. Monitoring and Observability  5.1 Distributed Tracing  Apollo uses OpenTelemetry for distributed tracing: - Trace Context: Propagated via W3C Trace Context headers - Sampling: Head-based sampling at 10% for production - Export: OTLP exporter sending to Jaeger  5.2 Metrics Collection  Key metrics collected from every service: - Request latency (p50, p95, p99) - Error rate (5xx responses / total requests) - Request rate (requests per second) - Resource usage (CPU, memory, GC pressure)  5.3 Alerting Rules

Critical alerts are configured in Prometheus: groups: - name: apollo-critical rules: - alert: HighErrorRate expr: error_rate > 0.05 for: 5m severity: critical - alert: ServiceDown expr: up == 0 for: 1m severity: critical  6. API Reference  6.1 REST Endpoints 6.2 WebSocket Events  Apollo supports real-time communication via WebSockets: - connection.established: Fired when a client connects - data.updated: Fired when subscribed data changes - notification.received: Push notifications to clients - error.occurred: Error events for client-side handling  6.3 Rate Limiting  API rate limits are enforced per tenant: - Free tier: 100 requests/minute - Pro tier: 1,000 requests/minute - Enterprise: 10,000 requests/minute (configurable) Rate limit headers are returned in every response: X-RateLimit-Limit: 1000 X-RateLimit-Remaining: 987 X-RateLimit-Reset: 1625097600

7. Performance Benchmarks  7.1 Latency Tests  Tests performed on a 3-node cluster (e2-standard-4):  7.2 Throughput  Under sustained load testing: - API Gateway: 15,000 requests/second per instance - Event Pipeline: 50,000 events/second per partition - Database Reads: 8,000 queries/second (with connection pooling) - Database Writes: 2,500 writes/second (with batch inserts)  8. Security Considerations  8.1 Encryption  - In Transit: TLS 1.3 for all HTTP traffic, mTLS for service-to-service - At Rest: AES-256 encryption for all data stores - Key Management: AWS KMS for key rotation and management  8.2 Access Control  Role-based access control (RBAC) with three built-in roles: - Admin: Full access to all resources - Developer: Read/write access to development resources - Viewer: Read-only access to monitoring and logs  8.3 Audit Logging  All security-relevant events are logged: - Authentication attempts (success and failure) - API key creation and revocation - Permission changes - Data export operations
9. Getting Started  9.1 Local Development  To set up a local development environment:
Clone the repository  git clone https://github.com/apollo/platform.git cd platform
Start development services  docker compose up -d
Run database migrations  npm run migrate
Start the development server  npm run dev  9.2 Configuration  Configuration is managed via environment variables:

Kafka  KAFKA_BROKERS=localhost:9092 KAFKA_GROUP_ID=apollo-processor  10. Troubleshooting Guide  10.1 Common Issues  Issue: Connection refused to database - Check if PostgreSQL is running: docker ps | grep postgres - Verify connection string in environment variables - Ensure network policies allow traffic on port 5432 Issue: JWT token expired - Tokens expire after 15 minutes - Use the refresh token endpoint to get a new token - Check system clock synchronization (NTP) Issue: High memory usage - Review the garbage collection logs - Increase heap size: --max-old-space-size=4096 - Check for memory leaks in event handlers  10.2 Debugging  Enable debug logging by setting the LOG_LEVEL environment variable: LOG_LEVEL=debug npm run dev Debug logs include: - Incoming request details - Database query execution plans - Event processing latency breakdowns - External API call timing
